title: Thomas Aquinas and the Dynamism of the Will in Choice
abstract: According to Aquinas, the moral quality of the human person depends on the actions carried out through the will, understood as efficient cause. In Aquinas’ thought, the will is a rational appetite, but it cannot be reduced to a mere executor of reason’s commands; otherwise, it would lack freedom, and its actions would not be meritorious. This raises a central question: how can the will be free in choosing its actions if it always chooses and acts under the guidance of reason? I argue that Aquinas provides sufficient theoretical resources to answer this question, resources that this article brings into focus.

title: A Thomistic Reply to Kant’s Practical Argument against Theoretical Knowledge of God and the Immortal Soul
abstract: This article evaluates whether Kant’s argument in §IX of the second Critique’s Dialectic—that theoretical knowledge of God and immortality would make the highest good practically impossible—would be convincing to a pre-Kantian “dogmatic” metaphysician who affirms this knowledge. Taking Aquinas as an exemplar of pre-Kantian metaphysics, I argue that Kant’s argument would not be convincing, since it turns out that Kant begs the question against the dogmatist. Kant therefore lacks this practical reason to reject knowledge of the unconditioned in favour of faith.

title: Concrete Intersubjectivity: Regarding the Analysis of Interaction
abstract: Interaction in the intersubjective sense, or the conceptual triangle including relationships and mutual communication, can also be named “concrete intersubjectivity.” A core of sociality, interactions can be personal, political, friendly, indifferent, intimate, casual, hostile, among humans, and among or with other animals, and are highly diverse, indeterminate, ambivalently multiple, and changeable. Yet they share a detailed structure, which implies their openness. Condensely: I. For Mia and Liu to interact is tantamount to Mia having some interactional engagement as in II. [I.i . . .] II. that Mia is interactionally engaged with Liu means that Mia is interactionally engaged-engaging as a whole person with Liu as a whole person interactionally engaged-engaging with herself as a whole person engaged-engaging with . . . [ad infinitum] [II.i. . .]. This second-personal analysis is both individualistic and relational, and identifies a logically ambivalent structure whose tension lies within interaction, and within subjectivity, rather than between them. This paper introduces my analysis of interaction or concrete intersubjectivity, or, again, of the relations between interaction and subjectivity.

title: Esse and Haecceitas: A Scotist-Existentialist Theory of Individuation
abstract: This paper proposes a two-level account of individuation, focusing on reconciling existential Thomism (especially Etienne Gilson’s version) and Duns Scotus’s teachings on haecceitas. I will argue, first, that the metaphysical root of individuation is the incommunicable act of existence (esse), that makes beings to actually exist and thus act in the world. Second, the formal specification of thisness within a finite individual is given by an intrinsic, non-qualitative principle. Following Scotus, I will call this haecceitas, “thisness.” The idea is to preserve the Thomistic priority of act over potency and the centrality of esse while addressing the concern that esse lacks the positive determination to secure thisness. The first section of the paper will discuss the views of Aquinas, Gilson, and Scotus in their historical context. The second section will attempt to synthesize Aquinas’s esse and Scotus haecceitas which, though having different roles, are essential to individuality. The third section will answer potential objections, followed by a concluding section.

title: Freedom, Love, and the Transcendentals: Toward an Integral Framework for Human Flourishing
abstract: This article investigates the metaphysical foundation for integral human flourishing through an updated account of the transcendentals based on the Thomistic tradition. Arguing that contemporary culture’s emphasis on autonomy neglects the intrinsically relational nature of being and fosters fragmentation, the text proposes that freedom attains its true purpose in loving relationships which reflect the structure of reality itself. Introducing relationality as a “pre-transcendental” provides an organizing principle for the transcendentals. “Integral thinking” is both based on and allows to give an account of the mutual interdependence of the transcendentals—each presupposing, enriching, and integrating with the others. This framework becomes fruitful in conjunction with the concept of “dynamic transcendentals” (Alice Ramos), the view that human beings are called to realize these principles actively in themselves and in the world through love, and thus offers a coherent basis for integral formation.

title: Kant and the Necessity of Imagination
abstract: It is well-known that Kant at some point thought the faculty of imagination was a necessary and autonomous faculty of the mind working in conjunction with sensibility and understanding to perform cognition. However, many scholars in the last 50 years have argued that Kant abandoned this view of the imagination and relegated its activities to the faculty of the understanding. In this paper, I join some recent scholars in pushing back against this view. I argue that the role of imagination in the Schematism chapter of the Critique of Pure Reason requires it to perform the necessary task of mediating between the non-conceptual intuitions of the sensibility and the concepts of the understanding. I argue that imagination cannot do this if it is the performance of the faculty of the understanding. Hence, it must be a faculty distinct from the understanding. I also explore how the schemata are initially generated by the imagination and later refined by the concepts of the understanding.

title: Understanding and Truth: Gadamer’s Hermeneutics in the Face of Relativistic Concerns
abstract: Gadamer’s hermeneutics has faced persistent objections regarding its alleged relativism. I explain why the problem of relativism arises in hermeneutics, while arguing that it is ultimately not entangled with relativism. I begin by proposing that the process of understanding, as construed in hermeneutics, involves critique, showing that hermeneutics is far from irrationalist relativism. However, the relativist objection is not dismissed by this alone. I argue that the difficulty stems from the fact that hermeneutics, like relativism, acknowledges the historical diversity of truth. To address this challenge, I examine three considerations: the character of understanding as a learning experience, the realist nature of hermeneutics, and the possibility of communication across heterogeneous perspectives. I then argue, in turn, that each of them, despite its significant contribution, remains insufficient on its own. I therefore introduce a further decisive element of Gadamer’s hermeneutics, namely truth, and finally show that, since truth, for Gadamer, is not a property of propositions or sentences but a finite event of unconcealment, the historical diversity of truth moves beyond the debate over relativism.

title: Marin Cureau de La Chambre’s Theory of Extension and Its Scholastic Background
abstract: By analysing his works from 1634 to 1666, I map out Cureau de La Chambre’s theory of universal extension. I focus in particular on what he borrowed from his contemporaries, especially his synthesis of Scaliger’s doctrine of the extension of spiritual substances and Suárez’s notion of extensio entitativa, a pre-categorial extension attributed to bodily parts prior to categorial continuous quantity. I reconstruct both his overall metaphysical account of extension and his specific positions on the extension of God, angels, and human souls, before outlining the main implications of this theory for his natural philosophy. I conclude with brief remarks on the possible relationship between La Chambre’s view and Henry More’s similar theory, set against Cureau’s anti-Cartesian polemic following the publication of Descartes’s letters in 1657.

title: Cruel Worlds and Counterfeit Natures: Why God Matters to Moral Realism
abstract: This paper explores the relationship between classical theism, natural law, and moral realism, focusing on the critical role of divine exemplarism in securing moral constraints. Drawing on the work of Bengson, Cuneo, and Shafer-Landau, the paper examines three central theses for a robust moral realism: (1) the existence of moral facts, (2) their stance-independence, and (3) the necessity of moral constraints. While Aristotelian essentialism addresses the first two theses by grounding moral truths in human nature, it falls short on the third—constraint. The paper argues that classical theism, through divine exemplarism, provides the necessary resources to secure moral constraints that other metaethical proposals lack. By asserting that all essences reflect divine goodness, theism constrains the moral landscape, ensuring moral facts are not arbitrary and preventing any world from permitting violations of essential moral platitudes—such as the idea that it could ever be good to torture, act cruelly, or slander for fun. The paper concludes that classical theism strengthens moral realism while offering a simpler, more robust explanation than alternative theories.

title: Pseudoscience: A Very Short Introduction by Michael D. Gordin
Hume’s Legacy and the Curate’s Egg
abstract: An important part of the Humean legacy in metaethics would be unacceptable to Hume himself, viz., the argument for moral non-cognitivism based on Humean moral psychology: This argument would be unacceptable to Hume because it requires the premise of motivational internalism: “Necessarily, if one judges an act right, then one is motivated to act in accordance with that judgment.” This asserts a universal, necessary connection between an agent’s moral judging and that agent’s being motivated to act accordingly, but this violates Hume’s claim that the only true propositions asserting universal, necessary connections are about “relations of ideas.” But motivational internalism cannot be about relations of ideas, because it fails the contradiction test in “Hume’s Fork.”

title: Suicide and Human Excellence
abstract: Increasing acceptance of assisted suicide in a post-God world demands consideration of the morality of suicide from a secular, non-theistic perspective. This essay offers such a consideration relying principally on Aristotle, his views on human excellence, suffering, happiness, and character. But any secular argument claiming support for a conclusion with binding force is likely to call to mind St. John Henry Newman’s famous objection that disbelief in the existence of God undermines any basis for the possibility of moral necessitation, a topic also addressed here. This essay provides the sketch of an argument developed in dialectical fashion, and like all competent dialectical arguments attends closely to the sort of person the hearer of the argument is, his disposition. In an argumentative setting, not only is the argument itself being put to the test but the participants are as well, and if the argument fails the fault may lie with the latter and not with the former.

title: The Question of Being or the Horizon of Love? Heidegger, Marion and the Possibility of Thinking God
abstract: In our times, Phenomenology brings challenge and opportunity to think about God. In this article, I examine the dependence of God on Being in Heidegger’s thought, focusing on three dimensions: the onto-theo-logical constitution of metaphysics, the pre-turn dependence of theology on philosophy, and the post-turn dependence of God on the truth of Beyng. Contrasting with this, I analyse how Marion critiques the subordination of God to Being as a form of idolatry, proposing instead that love, as self-giving, constitutes the proper horizon for thinking God. Then I explore how, within this horizon of love, “praise” emerges as a non-objectifying discourse that responds to God. Finally, I shall show that while Marion’s proposal is predicated on a strategic reading of Heidegger’s “Being,” this theoretical choice transcends Heidegger’s methodological agnosticism and opens a new phenomenological path for thinking the personal God.

title: On Coexistence: A Confucian-Daoist Perspective
abstract: This paper examines the problem of human coexistence through a complementary Confucian and Daoist lens. It explores how this combined perspective addresses three major challenges to coexistence. First is the risk of inherent abuse in the necessary institutionalization of coexistence as a vertical hierarchy. Second is the tension between partiality and equality. Third is the challenge that difference poses to unity. The paper argues that Xunzi’s analysis of sustaining a healthy hierarchical order offers an important response to the first challenge, that Mencius’s implicit insight into developing a notion of “ethical partiality” addresses the second, and that Zhuangzi’s philosophy of difference helps resolve the third.

title: Self and Other in Confucian Philosophy
abstract: It is commonly accepted that the “self” in Confucian thought is a relational one. What this means, however, is not always clear. In addition, the relationality of the “self” entails certain philosophical problems—such as how one delineates self from other, and how others contribute to the constitution of the self. This article’s investigation of the relational self in Confucian thought focuses on the concept of ren 仁, arguing that it represents a bidimensional social ontology that connects horizontal and vertical dimensions between self and particular and general others. It focuses on the transmitted version of the character for ren, composed of “person and two,” to discuss the horizontal dimension, and on the Guodian excavated version, composed of “body above mind below,” to discuss the vertical dimension. Furthermore, it introduces George H. Mead’s theory of the “generalized other” to elaborate on the vertical dimensionality of the self-other relationship. This will show that self and other are mutually produced and incorporated into a progressive sociocultural complex of selfhood.
